data.name: IntroText
data.value: Here you will find the latest course news, information about upcoming training sessions and training suggestions.

data.name: WelcomeText
data.value: Hello {0}, here is your current training status.

data.name: TrainingOverview_Empty
data.value: There is no upcoming session planned right now.

data.name: TrainingOverview_PreviewText
data.value: This area can later show a timeline of sessions, load, or progress.

data.name: ReferencePanel_Empty
data.value: No assessment run has been saved yet.

data.name: Metric_RunningAnalysisInfoDetail
data.value: Why we do not correct every pattern

data.name: MentalResource_Title
data.value: Why running is more than training

data.name: MentalResource_Lead
data.value: Running does not only train muscles and circulation. For many people it becomes a controllable moment in the day: a tool that steadies mood, lowers tension, and makes self-efficacy tangible.

data.name: MentalResource_StepEffectText
data.value: Reviews suggest that single running bouts and regular recreational running can support mood and mental health. The evidence has limits, but it is strong enough to treat running as a psychological resource.

data.name: MentalResource_ResearchIntro
data.value: The current literature suggests that running can be a low-threshold mental resource.

data.name: MentalResource_ResearchOswald
data.value: A scoping review on running and mental health shows that single running bouts and regular running are associated with better mood, lower tension, and positive mental health outcomes.

data.name: MentalResource_ResearchPereira
data.value: A systematic review on recreational running adds that motivation, self-regulation, habit, and perceived competence are central psychological factors for staying with running.

data.name: MentalResource_ResearchActivityReviews
data.value: Broader reviews support this interpretation: physical activity reduces depressive symptoms, anxiety, and psychological distress, and walking/jogging is among the well-studied forms of movement for depressive symptoms.

data.name: MentalResource_ResearchCochrane
data.value: The current Cochrane review also concludes that exercise can moderately reduce depressive symptoms compared with control conditions, while long-term evidence remains limited.

data.name: MentalResource_ResearchProspective
data.value: Prospective cohort data also show that even moderate activity volumes are associated with a lower risk of depression.

data.name: MentalResource_ResearchConclusion
data.value: We do not want to inflate running into therapy or a therapy substitute. The point is that running can be a mental resource and that many people intuitively use it as one.

data.name: MentalResource_StepFragileText
data.value: As a resource, running depends on load tolerance, recovery, and everyday constraints. Pain, overload, or time pressure can interrupt access; then people lose not only training but also a practiced form of self-regulation.

data.name: MentalResource_StepRiskTitle
data.value: Where risk is higher for beginners

data.name: MentalResource_StepRiskText
data.value: Reported injury incidence is markedly higher in novice runners than in experienced recreational runners. In beginner programs, a meaningful share also stops early, often because of pain or injury.

data.name: MentalResource_StepConsequenceText
data.value: Sustainable running programs therefore cannot treat drop-out as a side issue. When frustration, embarrassment, or guilt blocks a return, more than a training plan has failed: an everyday resource has been lost.

data.name: MentalResource_StepAppText
data.value: PaceLetics starts there: dose load, make routines understandable, protect progress over the long term, and enable athletes to make their own decisions.

data.name: MentalResource_SourceOswald
data.value: Oswald et al. Running and mental health: A scoping review. Int J Environ Res Public Health. 2020.

data.name: MentalResource_SourcePereira
data.value: Pereira et al. Psychological and behavioral correlates of recreational running. Front Psychol. 2021.

data.name: MentalResource_SourceSingh
data.value: Singh et al. Effectiveness of physical activity interventions for improving depression, anxiety and distress. Br J Sports Med. 2023.

data.name: MentalResource_SourceNoetel
data.value: Noetel et al. Effect of exercise for depression: systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials. BMJ. 2024.

data.name: MentalResource_SourceCochrane
data.value: Cochrane Review. Exercise for depression. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2026.

data.name: MentalResource_SourcePearce
data.value: Pearce et al. Association between physical activity and risk of depression. JAMA Psychiatry. 2022.

data.name: MentalResource_SourceFurie
data.value: Furie et al. Mental health and involuntary retirement from sports after musculoskeletal injury. Curr Rev Musculoskelet Med. 2023.

data.name: MentalResource_SourceDeJonge
data.value: de Jonge et al. Mental recovery and running-related injuries in recreational runners. Int J Environ Res Public Health. 2020.

data.name: MentalResource_SourceVerhagen
data.value: Verhagen et al. "I just want to run": how recreational runners perceive and deal with injuries. BMJ Open Sport Exerc Med. 2021.

data.name: MentalResource_SourceVidebaek
data.value: Videbaek et al. Incidence of running-related injuries per 1000 h of running. Sports Med. 2015.

data.name: MentalResource_SourceFokkema
data.value: Fokkema et al. Reasons and predictors of discontinuation after a novice running program. J Sci Med Sport. 2019.

data.name: MentalResource_SourceRelph
data.value: Relph et al. Couch-to-5k or Couch to Ouch to Couch? Int J Environ Res Public Health. 2023.

data.name: PartialLoadError
data.value: Some dashboard data could not be loaded. Existing information is still shown.
